0.title: Knowledge Hub Foundations
0.abstract: Knowledge Hub Foundations curates the first expansion wave of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records around graph systems, preservation, generative systems, signal, audio and public semantic publishing.

1.title: Knowledge Hub Fourth Wave
1.abstract: Knowledge Hub Fourth Wave groups articles and technology records about MCP, C2PA, WebNN, IIIF, OpenTelemetry, AI search, governance, prompt injection and verifiable identity.

2.title: Knowledge Hub Second Wave
2.abstract: Knowledge Hub Second Wave groups a larger expansion of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records across hypertext, open source, metadata, creative coding, web audio, motion, typography, HCI, algorithmic music and generative AI.

3.title: Knowledge Hub Third Wave
3.abstract: Knowledge Hub Third Wave groups a major expansion across LLMs, AI agents, local AI, RAG, multimodal systems, systems thinking, ontologies, graph modeling, event streams, Redis, microservices and domain runtimes.

4.title: AI Agent
4.definition: An AI agent is a software system in which a model can select actions, use tools, observe results and adapt its next steps toward an objective within defined boundaries.
4.abstract: AI agents combine models, tools, memory, environmental feedback, stopping conditions, permissions and evaluation in an iterative action loop.

5.title: Algorithmic Composition
5.definition: Algorithmic composition is the use of rules, procedures, probability, computation or formal systems to generate or organize musical material.
5.abstract: Algorithmic composition connects music theory, computation, cybernetics, generative systems and electronic music by treating composition as the design of procedures as well as sounds.

6.title: Augmented Intelligence
6.definition: Augmented intelligence is the design of tools, interfaces and systems that expand human perception, reasoning, memory and creative capability.
6.abstract: Augmented intelligence reframes computation as a partner in human work, linking HCI, AI, knowledge systems, creative tools and Electronic Artefacts runtime research.

7.title: Autonomous System
7.definition: An autonomous system can perceive relevant state, select actions and adapt behavior toward objectives with limited direct human control during operation.
7.abstract: Autonomous systems combine sensing, models, planning, execution, feedback, constraints, governance and human intervention paths.

8.title: Contextual Execution
8.definition: Contextual execution is the practice of making identity, state, permissions, relationships and operating context active constraints on what a system can do.
8.abstract: Contextual execution describes runtime behavior where an operation is evaluated through entity identity, graph relations, permissions, temporal state and intended scope.

9.title: Creative Coding
9.definition: Creative coding is the use of programming as an artistic, design or exploratory medium rather than only as an engineering implementation tool.
9.abstract: Creative coding connects software, visual culture, sound, interaction, generative systems, education and experimental publishing.

10.title: Cybernetic Feedback
10.definition: Cybernetic feedback is the circulation of information about a system's state back into the system so that behavior can be regulated, adapted or transformed.
10.abstract: Cybernetic feedback connects control, communication, systems theory, generative art, machine listening, adaptive interfaces and runtime governance.

11.title: Digital Preservation
11.definition: Digital preservation is the long-term work of keeping digital objects understandable, authentic, usable and accessible despite changes in formats, software, hardware and context.
11.abstract: Digital preservation connects file formats, metadata, provenance, storage, rights, migration and interpretation into a long-term care practice for digital culture.

12.title: Domain Operating System
12.definition: A domain operating system is a shared runtime and information layer that coordinates identities, entities, permissions, workflows, events and interfaces for a specific field of activity.
12.abstract: A domain operating system turns repeated domain rules into reusable infrastructure without claiming to replace a general-purpose computer operating system.

13.title: Entity Identity
13.definition: Entity identity is the durable naming of a person, concept, project, program, publication or artefact so that references remain stable as descriptions evolve.
13.abstract: Entity identity gives knowledge systems a way to cite things without confusing names, pages, files, versions and changing descriptions.

14.title: Event-Driven Architecture
14.definition: Event-driven architecture is a system design approach in which components publish, transport and react to records of state change rather than depending only on direct synchronous calls.
14.abstract: Event-driven architecture connects event producers, brokers or logs, consumers, schemas, delivery semantics, projections and observability.

15.title: Generative AI
15.definition: Generative AI refers to machine-learning systems that produce or transform media such as text, image, audio, video, code or structured data from learned patterns.
15.abstract: Generative AI connects machine learning, creative workflows, latent spaces, risk management, provenance, authorship and cultural production.

16.title: Generative System
16.definition: A generative system is a rule-based, procedural or computational arrangement that produces variable outputs from defined constraints, materials and processes.
16.abstract: Generative systems connect creative coding, cybernetics, algorithmic composition, simulation, design tools and AI by shifting authorship toward rules, feedback and selection.

17.title: Graph Modeling
17.definition: Graph modeling represents a domain through addressable nodes, typed relationships and properties chosen to answer specific questions and support specific operations.
17.abstract: Graph modeling makes many-to-many relationships, paths, provenance and evolving context explicit where folders and rigid trees obscure them.

18.title: Graph Runtime
18.definition: A graph runtime is an execution environment in which addressable entities and typed relationships participate directly in contextual computation.
18.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses graph runtime to describe systems where entities, relationships, identity, context and events form an executable structure rather than a passive data model.

19.title: Human Computer Interaction
19.definition: Human computer interaction is the study and design of relationships between people, computational systems, interfaces, tasks, environments and cultures of use.
19.abstract: HCI connects interface design, accessibility, creative tools, augmented intelligence, software ergonomics and the social consequences of computation.

20.title: Hypertext
20.definition: Hypertext is a system of non-linear text and media connected by links, allowing readers and machines to traverse knowledge through associations.
20.abstract: Hypertext is a foundational concept for the web, digital archives, knowledge graphs, digital gardens, public notes and Electronic Artefacts knowledge publishing.

21.title: Internet Culture
21.definition: Internet culture is the set of social forms, aesthetics, protocols, communities, memes, archives and practices that emerge through networked communication.
21.abstract: Internet culture connects network protocols, web publishing, platform communities, memes, archives, open source, digital folklore and public knowledge systems.

22.title: Knowledge Graph
22.definition: A knowledge graph is a structured network of entities, identifiers and typed relationships that makes knowledge navigable, queryable and reusable across contexts.
22.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses knowledge graph to describe public knowledge systems where concepts, projects, programs, publications, sources and artefacts keep stable identities and typed relations.

23.title: Large Language Model
23.definition: A large language model is a parameterized machine-learning model trained to predict and generate sequences of tokens from statistical patterns learned across large text or multimodal corpora.
23.abstract: Large language models combine tokenization, embeddings, transformer layers, attention, training objectives and probabilistic decoding to generate or transform language.

24.title: Linked Data
24.definition: Linked Data is a web publishing approach that identifies things with stable URIs and connects them through machine-readable statements.
24.abstract: Linked Data gives public knowledge systems a way to make entities, pages, references and datasets interoperable across sites and software systems.

25.title: Metadata
25.definition: Metadata is structured information about a resource, such as title, creator, date, format, source, rights, subject, relation and preservation context.
25.abstract: Metadata is the descriptive, administrative, structural and preservation layer that makes archives, catalogues, digital objects and knowledge graphs usable over time.

26.title: Microservice Architecture
26.definition: Microservice architecture structures a software system as independently deployable services aligned with bounded capabilities and communicating through explicit contracts.
26.abstract: Microservices can improve independent evolution and scaling but add network, data, deployment, observability and organizational complexity.

27.title: Motion Design
27.definition: Motion design is the use of time, movement, transition, rhythm and sequencing to structure visual communication and interface behavior.
27.abstract: Motion design connects visual culture, interface semantics, typography, animation, sound, choreography and web interaction.

28.title: Multimodal AI
28.definition: Multimodal AI describes systems that learn from, align, transform or generate more than one modality such as text, image, audio, video, sensor data or structured records.
28.abstract: Multimodal AI connects representation learning, modality encoders, shared embedding spaces, generation, alignment and cross-modal interfaces.

29.title: Ontology
29.definition: In knowledge engineering, an ontology is an explicit specification of the kinds of entities, properties, relationships and constraints used to represent a domain.
29.abstract: Ontologies make domain meaning explicit and interoperable, while taxonomies organize concepts primarily through classification and hierarchy.

30.title: Open Source
30.definition: Open source is a software and cultural practice in which source code is made available under licenses that allow use, study, modification and redistribution.
30.abstract: Open source is both a licensing model and a cultural infrastructure for software, creative tools, public knowledge, maintainership and technical education.

31.title: Open-Weight Model
31.definition: An open-weight model is a machine-learning model whose learned parameters are distributed for reuse under stated terms, without necessarily providing the training data, training code or freedoms required by open-source definitions.
31.abstract: Open-weight models enable local inference and adaptation but must be distinguished from fully open-source AI systems.

32.title: Personal Knowledge System
32.definition: A personal knowledge system is a practice and tool environment for capturing, linking, revising and retrieving notes, sources, ideas and decisions over time.
32.abstract: Personal knowledge systems connect digital gardens, wikis, note archives, research methods, backlinks and long-term intellectual memory.

33.title: Procedural Graphics
33.definition: Procedural graphics are images or visual systems generated from rules, algorithms, parameters, simulation or code rather than only manually drawn assets.
33.abstract: Procedural graphics connect shaders, generative systems, motion design, creative coding, visual culture and real-time web interfaces.

34.title: Provenance
34.definition: Provenance is information about the people, activities, sources, transformations and decisions involved in producing a thing or piece of data.
34.abstract: Provenance helps readers assess reliability, rights, authorship, lineage, context and trust across research, archive, software and cultural records.

35.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
35.definition: Retrieval-augmented generation is an architecture that retrieves external information at query time and supplies selected evidence to a generative model before or during response production.
35.abstract: RAG connects search, embeddings, document segmentation, metadata, provenance, context assembly, language models and answer evaluation.

36.title: Signal Archaeology
36.definition: Signal archaeology is the interpretation of technical, sonic, visual or archival traces as evidence of hidden processes, transformations and histories.
36.abstract: Signal archaeology gives Electronic Artefacts a way to connect audio analysis, media residue, archival provenance, memory and cultural interpretation.

37.title: Systems Thinking
37.definition: Systems thinking is an approach that studies elements through their relationships, feedback, boundaries, delays, stocks, flows and patterns of behavior over time.
37.abstract: Systems thinking helps creators and technologists understand why local interventions can produce indirect, delayed or counterintuitive effects.

38.title: Typography
38.definition: Typography is the design and arrangement of written language through typefaces, spacing, hierarchy, rhythm, scale and reading conditions.
38.abstract: Typography connects design, reading systems, accessibility, visual culture, web interfaces and long-form knowledge publishing.

39.title: Web Audio
39.definition: Web audio is the use of browser-based audio APIs and related web technologies to synthesize, process, analyze, route and present sound.
39.abstract: Web audio connects sound design, browser runtimes, interactive media, creative coding and public sound tools.

40.title: Fondations du hub de connaissance
40.abstract: Knowledge Hub Foundations curates the first expansion wave de Electronic Artefacts knowledge records around graphe systèmes, preservation, generative systèmes, signal, audio et public sémantique publishing.

41.title: Hub de connaissance, quatrième vague
41.abstract: Knowledge Hub Fourth Wave groups articles et technology records about MCP, C2PA, WebNN, IIIF, OpenTelemetry, AI search, gouvernance, prompt injection et verifiable identité.

42.title: Hub de connaissance, deuxième vague
42.abstract: Knowledge Hub Second Wave groups a larger expansion de Electronic Artefacts knowledge records across hypertext, open source, métadonnées, programmation créative, web audio, motion, typographey, HCI, algorithmic music et generative AI.

43.title: Hub de connaissance, troisième vague
43.abstract: Knowledge Hub Third Wave groups a major expansion across LLMs, AI agents, local AI, RAG, multimodal systèmes, systèmes thinking, ontologies, graphe modèleing, event streams, Redis, microservices et domain runtimes.

44.title: Agent d’IA
44.definition: An AI agent est un software système dans which a modèle can select actions, use outils, observe results et adapt its next steps toward an objective within defined boundaries.
44.abstract: AI agents combine modèles, outils, mémoire, environmental feedback, stopping conditions, permissions et evaluation dans an iterative action loop.

45.title: Composition algorithmique
45.definition: Algorithmic composition est le use de rules, procedures, probability, computation ou formal systèmes à generate ou organize musical material.
45.abstract: Algorithmic composition relie music theory, computation, cybernetics, generative systèmes et electronic music by treating composition comme the design de procedures comme well comme sounds.

46.title: Intelligence augmentée
46.definition: Augmented intelligence est le design de outils, interfaces et systèmes that expand human perception, reasoning, mémoire et creative capability.
46.abstract: Augmented intelligence reframes computation comme a partner dans human work, linking HCI, AI, systèmes de connaissance, creative outils et Electronic Artefacts runtime recherche.

47.title: Système autonome
47.definition: An autonomous système can perceive relevant state, select actions et adapt behavior toward objectives avec limited direct human control during operation.
47.abstract: Autonomous systèmes combine sensing, modèles, planning, execution, feedback, constraints, gouvernance et human intervention paths.

48.title: Exécution contextuelle
48.definition: Contextual execution est le practice de making identité, state, permissions, relationships et operating context active constraints on what a système can do.
48.abstract: Contextual execution décrit runtime behavior where an operation is evaluated through entité identité, graphe relations, permissions, temporal state et intended scope.

49.title: Programmation créative
49.definition: Creative coding est le use de programming comme an artistic, design ou exploratory medium rather than only comme an engineering implementation outil.
49.abstract: Creative coding relie software, visuel culture, sound, interaction, generative systèmes, education et experimental publishing.

50.title: Rétroaction cybernétique
50.definition: Cybernetic feedback est le circulation de information about a système's state back into the système so that behavior can be regulated, adapted ou transformed.
50.abstract: Cybernetic feedback relie control, communication, systèmes theory, generative art, machine listening, adaptive interfaces et runtime gouvernance.

51.title: Préservation numérique
51.definition: Digital preservation est le long-term work de keeping digital objects understandable, authentic, usable et accessible despite changes dans formats, software, hardware et context.
51.abstract: Digital preservation relie file formats, métadonnées, provenance, storage, rights, migration et interpretation into a long-term care practice pour digital culture.

52.title: Système d’exploitation de domaine
52.definition: A domain operating système est un shared runtime et information layer that coordinates identités, entités, permissions, workflows, events et interfaces pour a specific field de activity.
52.abstract: A domain operating système turns repeated domain rules into reusable infrastructure without claiming à replace a general-purpose computer operating système.

53.title: Identité d’entité
53.definition: Entité identité est le durable naming de a person, concept, project, program, publication ou artefact so that references remain stable comme descriptions evolve.
53.abstract: Entité identité gives systèmes de connaissance a way à cite things without confusing names, pages, files, versions et changing descriptions.

54.title: Architecture événementielle
54.definition: Event-driven architecture est un système design approach dans which components publish, transport et react à records de state change rather than depending only on direct synchronous calls.
54.abstract: Event-driven architecture relie event producers, brokers ou logs, consumers, schemas, delivery sémantiques, projections et observability.

55.title: IA générative
55.definition: Generative AI refers à machine-learning systèmes that produce ou transform médias such comme text, image, audio, video, code ou structured données from learned patterns.
55.abstract: Generative AI relie machine learning, creative workflows, latent spaces, risk management, provenance, authorship et culturel production.

56.title: Système génératif
56.definition: A generative système est un rule-based, procedural ou computational arrangement that produces variable outputs from defined constraints, materials et processes.
56.abstract: Generative systèmes connect programmation créative, cybernetics, algorithmic composition, simulation, design outils et AI by shifting authorship toward rules, feedback et selection.

57.title: Modélisation de graphe
57.definition: Graphe modèleing represents a domain through addressable nodes, typed relationships et properties chosen à answer specific questions et support specific operations.
57.abstract: Graphe modèleing makes many-à-many relationships, paths, provenance et evolving context explicit where folders et rigid trees obscure them.

58.title: Runtime de graphe
58.definition: A graphe runtime est un execution environment dans which addressable entités et typed relationships participate directly dans contextual computation.
58.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses graphe runtime à describe systèmes where entités, relationships, identité, context et events form an executable structure rather than a passive données modèle.

59.title: Human Computer Interaction
59.definition: Human computer interaction est le study et design de relationships between people, computational systèmes, interfaces, tasks, environments et cultures de use.
59.abstract: HCI relie interface design, accessibility, creative outils, augmented intelligence, software ergonomics et the social consequences de computation.

60.title: Hypertexte
60.definition: Hypertext est un système de non-linear text et médias connected by links, allowing readers et machines à traverse knowledge through associations.
60.abstract: Hypertext est un foundational concept pour the web, digital archives, graphee de connaissancess, digital gardens, public notes et Electronic Artefacts knowledge publishing.

61.title: Culture Internet
61.definition: Internet culture est le set de social forms, aesthetics, protocoles, communities, memes, archives et practices that emerge through networked communication.
61.abstract: Internet culture relie network protocoles, web publishing, plateforme communities, memes, archives, open source, digital folklore et public systèmes de connaissance.

62.title: Graphe de connaissances
62.definition: A graphee de connaissances est un structured network de entités, identifiers et typed relationships that makes knowledge navigable, queryable et reusable across contexts.
62.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses graphee de connaissances à describe public systèmes de connaissance where concepts, projects, programs, publications, sources et artefacts keep stable identités et typed relations.

63.title: Grand modèle de langage
63.definition: A large language modèle est un parameterized machine-learning modèle trained à predict et generate sequences de tokens from statistical patterns learned across large text ou multimodal corpora.
63.abstract: Large language modèles combine tokenization, embeddings, transformer layers, attention, training objectives et probabilistic decoding à generate ou transform language.

64.title: Données liées
64.definition: Données liées est un web publishing approach that identifies things avec stable URIs et relie them through machine-readable statements.
64.abstract: Données liées gives public systèmes de connaissance a way à make entités, pages, references et donnéessets interoperable across sites et software systèmes.

65.title: Métadonnées
65.definition: Métadonnées is structured information about a resource, such comme title, creator, date, format, source, rights, subject, relation et preservation context.
65.abstract: Métadonnées est le descriptive, administrative, structural et preservation layer that makes archives, catalogues, digital objects et graphee de connaissancess usable over time.

66.title: Architecture de microservices
66.definition: Microservice architecture structures a software système comme independently deployable services aligned avec bounded capabilities et communicating through explicit contracts.
66.abstract: Microservices can improve independent evolution et scaling but add network, données, deployment, observability et organizational complexity.

67.title: Motion design
67.definition: Motion design est le use de time, movement, transition, rhythm et sequencing à structure visuel communication et interface behavior.
67.abstract: Motion design relie visuel culture, interface sémantiques, typographey, animation, sound, choreographey et web interaction.

68.title: IA multimodale
68.definition: Multimodal AI décrit systèmes that learn from, align, transform ou generate more than one modality such comme text, image, audio, video, sensor données ou structured records.
68.abstract: Multimodal AI relie representation learning, modality encoders, shared embedding spaces, generation, alignment et cross-modal interfaces.

69.title: Ontologie
69.definition: In knowledge engineering, an ontology est un explicit specification de the kinds de entités, properties, relationships et constraints used à represent a domain.
69.abstract: Ontologies make domain meaning explicit et interoperable, while taxonomies organize concepts primarily through classification et hierarchy.

70.title: Open source
70.definition: Open source est un software et culturel practice dans which source code is made available under licenses that allow use, study, modification et redistribution.
70.abstract: Open source is both a licensing modèle et a culturel infrastructure pour software, creative outils, public knowledge, maintainership et technical education.

71.title: Open-Weight Model
71.definition: An open-weight modèle est un machine-learning modèle whose learned parameters sont distributed pour reuse under stated terms, without necessarily providing the training données, training code ou freedoms required by open-source definitions.
71.abstract: Open-weight modèles enable local inference et adaptation but must be distinguished from fully open-source AI systèmes.

72.title: Système personnel de connaissance
72.definition: A personal knowledge système est un practice et outil environment pour capturing, linking, revising et retrieving notes, sources, ideas et decisions over time.
72.abstract: Personal systèmes de connaissance connect digital gardens, wikis, note archives, recherche méthodes, backlinks et long-term intellectual mémoire.

73.title: Graphisme procédural
73.definition: Procedural grapheics sont images ou visuel systèmes generated from rules, algorithms, parameters, simulation ou code rather than only manually drawn assets.
73.abstract: Procedural grapheics connect shaders, generative systèmes, motion design, programmation créative, visuel culture et real-time web interfaces.

74.title: Provenance
74.definition: Provenance is information about the people, activities, sources, transformations et decisions involved dans producing a thing ou piece de données.
74.abstract: Provenance helps readers assess reliability, rights, authorship, lineage, context et trust across recherche, archive, software et culturel records.

75.title: Génération augmentée par récupération
75.definition: Retrieval-augmented generation est un architecture that retrieves external information at query time et supplies selected evidence à a generative modèle before ou during response production.
75.abstract: RAG relie search, embeddings, document segmentation, métadonnées, provenance, context assembly, language modèles et answer evaluation.

76.title: Archéologie du signal
76.definition: Signal archaeology est le interpretation de technical, sonic, visuel ou archival traces comme evidence de hidden processes, transformations et histories.
76.abstract: Signal archaeology gives Electronic Artefacts a way à connect audio analysis, médias residue, archival provenance, mémoire et culturel interpretation.

77.title: Pensée systémique
77.definition: Systèmes thinking est un approach that studies elements through their relationships, feedback, boundaries, delays, stocks, flows et patterns de behavior over time.
77.abstract: Systèmes thinking helps creators et technologists understand why local interventions can produce indirect, delayed ou counterintuitive effects.

78.title: Typographie
78.definition: Typographey est le design et arrangement de written language through typefaces, spacing, hierarchy, rhythm, scale et reading conditions.
78.abstract: Typographey relie design, reading systèmes, accessibility, visuel culture, web interfaces et long-form knowledge publishing.

79.title: Audio web
79.definition: Web audio est le use de navigateur-based audio APIs et related web technologies à synthesize, process, analyze, route et present sound.
79.abstract: Web audio relie sound design, navigateur runtimes, interactive médias, programmation créative et public sound outils.

80.title: Electronic Artefacts
80.abstract: Electronic Artefacts est un studio indépendant qui conçoit des produits numériques, des systèmes de connaissance, des plateformes culturelles et des expériences issues de la recherche.

81.title: ORETH
81.abstract: ORETH est un programme expérimental consacré à l’observation, l’analyse et la transformation de phénomènes sonores complexes par le traitement du signal, l’apprentissage automatique et la création musicale.

82.title: VASTE
82.abstract: VASTE est le principal moteur propriétaire développé et commercialisé par Electronic Artefacts pour les architectures graphiques, l’exécution contextuelle, les systèmes d’identité et la construction de mondes modulaires.

83.title: Palimpsests
83.abstract: Palimpsests est un cycle conceptuel d’ORETH explorant mémoire, héritage, transformation et transmission à travers la composition sonore, l’image et la publication.

84.title: Vestiges
84.abstract: Vestiges est la plateforme principale fondée sur VASTE pour cartographier, préserver et activer les savoir-faire vivants à travers les personnes, pratiques, matériaux, lieux et institutions.

85.title: Agents d’IA et workflows d’IA
85.abstract: A practical comparison de modèle-directed agents, deterministic workflows, hybrid orchestration, outil permissions, mémoire, evaluation et human oversight.

86.title: Recherche IA, contenu structuré et SEO par graphe de connaissances
86.abstract: A practical article on AI search visibility, structured content, JSON-LD, entité identité, graphee de connaissances SEO et non-commodity editorial authority.

87.title: Composition algorithmique et musique fondée sur des règles
87.abstract: This article explains algorithmic composition through rules, stochastic méthodes, cybernetics, electronic music, programmation créative, generative systèmes et ORETH.

88.title: C2PA, Content Credentials et provenance des médias génératifs
88.abstract: A practical explanation de C2PA, Content Credentials, signed médias manifests, provenance claims, trust limits et archive use.

89.title: Exécution contextuelle et runtimes de graphe
89.abstract: This article explains contextual execution comme a runtime principle pour systèmes where identité, relations, permissions et state influence what operations can occur.

90.title: Pédagogie du creative coding, de Logo à p5.js
90.abstract: This article explains programmation créative pedagogy through exploratory programming, computational literacy, Processing, p5.js, visuel feedback et open learning communities.

91.title: Préservation numérique et archives vivantes
91.abstract: This article explains préservation numérique comme an active culturel practice that keeps digital objects, métadonnées, provenance et interpretation usable over time.

92.title: Architecture événementielle et flux d’événements
92.abstract: A practical explanation de events, commands, queues, logs, brokers, schemas, delivery sémantiques, idempotency, projections, replay et observability.

93.title: Foundational Lineage #001
93.abstract: This recherche note records early architecture, communication-système et documentation threads that led à Electronic Artefacts, Runtime Theory et VASTE.

94.title: IA générative, espace latent et workflows créatifs
94.abstract: This article explains generative AI comme a creative workflow topic, covering latent spaces, prompts, provenance, risk, evaluation, authorship et Electronic Artefacts systèmes.

95.title: Systèmes génératifs, cybernétique et programmation créative
95.abstract: This article traces generative systèmes from cybernetic feedback et algorithmic art à programmation créative, sound design et contemporary AI-assisted production.

96.title: Comment fonctionnent vraiment les grands modèles de langage
96.abstract: A grounded explanation de how large language modèles turn tokens into predictions through embeddings, transformer layers, attention, training et probabilistic inference.

97.title: Human Computer Interaction for Creative Tools
97.abstract: This article explains HCI pour creative outils, focusing on feedback, agency, flow, error recovery, accessibility, augmented intelligence et computational médias.

98.title: Hypertexte et systèmes de connaissance augmentée
98.abstract: This article explains hypertext comme a long-term knowledge architecture, connecting Vannevar Bush, Douglas Engelbart, web links, graphee de connaissancess et augmented intelligence.

99.title: IIIF et objets culturels composés
99.abstract: A practical explanation de IIIF Presentation API concepts, compound objects, manifests, canvases, annotations et culturel archive interfaces.

100.title: Graphes de connaissances pour les infrastructures culturelles
100.abstract: This article explains how graphee de connaissancess support culturel infrastructure by giving people, works, sources, concepts, projects et archives stable identités et explicit relations.

101.title: Données liées et pages publiques de connaissance
101.abstract: This article explains how public knowledge pages can use stable URLs, JSON-LD, RDF-oriented identifiers et internal links à become useful à humans, search engines et AI systèmes.

102.title: Systèmes d’IA locaux et open source
102.abstract: A practical guide à local inference, open-weight modèles, open-source AI, quantization, hardware, privacy, licensing et operational independence.

103.title: Métadonnées, catalogage et mémoire culturelle
103.abstract: This article explains métadonnées comme a culturel mémoire layer, connecting catalogage, Dublin Core, PREMIS, METS, provenance, archives et graphee de connaissancess.

104.title: Microservices, monolithes modulaires et frontières de système
104.abstract: A pragmatic comparison de microservices et modular monoliths through domain boundaries, données ownership, deployment, events, observability et team structure.

105.title: Model Context Protocol et systèmes d’IA utilisant des outils
105.abstract: A practical explanation de MCP comme an integration protocole pour resources, prompts, outils, consent, host boundaries et graphe-scoped AI workflows.

106.title: Motion design, temps et sémantique d’interface
106.abstract: This article explains motion design comme a sémantique layer pour digital interfaces, connecting time, transition, attention, accessibility et Web Animations.

107.title: IA multimodale pour le texte, l’image, l’audio et la vidéo
107.abstract: A durable guide à multimodal AI architecture, shared representations, cross-modal retrieval, generation, temporal médias, provenance et creative applications.

108.title: Observabilité des agents d’IA et des systèmes d’appel d’outils
108.abstract: A practical explanation de telemetry pour AI agents, outil calls, traces, events, approvals, failures, evaluations et graphe-runtime accountability.

109.title: Ontologies, taxonomies et modélisation des connaissances
109.abstract: A practical guide à taxonomies, thesauri, folksonomies, ontologies, controlled vocabularies, OWL, SKOS et sustainable sémantique modèleing.

110.title: L’open source comme infrastructure culturelle
110.abstract: This article frames open source comme culturel infrastructure, explaining licenses, maintenance, creative outils, dependency chains, commons et public systèmes de connaissance.

111.title: Systèmes personnels de connaissance et jardins numériques
111.abstract: This article explains personal systèmes de connaissance et digital gardens comme durable practices pour notes, sources, backlinks, revision, public learning et recherche libraries.

112.title: Graphisme procédural, shaders et systèmes visuels
